Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - A MODULATION LAYER TO INCREASE NEURAL NET WORK ROBUSTNESS AGAINST DATA QUALITY ISSUES

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Data quality is a common problem in machine learning, especially in high-stakes settings such as healthcare. Missing data affects accuracy, calibration, and feature attribution in complex patterns. Developers often train models on carefully curated datasets to minimize missing data bias; however, this reduces the usability of such models in production environments, such as real-time healthcare records. Making machine learning models robust to missing data is therefore crucial for practical applications. While some classifiers naturally handle missing data, others, such as deep neural networks, are not designed for unknown values. We propose a novel neural network modification to mitigate the impacts of missing data. The approach is inspired by neuromodulation that is performed by biological neural networks. Our proposal replaces the fixed weights of a fully-connected layer with a function of an additional input (reliability score) at each input, mimicking the ability of cortex to up- and down-weight inputs based on the presence of other data. The modulation function is jointly learned with the main task using a multilayer perceptron. We tested our modulating fully connected layer on multiple classification, regression, and imputation problems, and it either improved performance or generated comparable performance to conventional neural network architectures concatenating reliability to the inputs. Models with modulating layers were more robust against degradation of data quality by introducing additional missingness at evaluation time. These results suggest that explicitly accounting for reduced information quality with a modulating fully connected layer can enable the deployment of artificial intelligence systems in real-time settings.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Despite the enormous academic and industrial interest in artificial intelligence, there is a large gap between model performance in laboratory settings and real-world deployments. Reports estimate that over 75% of data science and artificial intelligence projects do not make it into production (VentureBeat, 2019; Sagar, 2021; Chen and Asch, 2017). One difficult transition from the laboratory is handling noisy and missing data. Errors in predictor data and labels (Northcutt et al., 2021) at the training stage are well understood to produce poor pattern recognition with any strategy; garbage-in garbage-out. In the statistical learning literature, the effects of inaccurate and missing data on simple classifiers such as logistic regression is particularly well understood (Ameisen, 2020). As a result, datasets intended to train high-accuracy models are often carefully curated and reviewed for validity (Ameisen, 2020; Xiao et al., 2018). However, when faced with noisy data from a new source, these models may fail (L’Heureux et al., 2017). One special case is convolutional neural networks for machine vision; augmenting the dataset with partially obscured inputs has been shown to increase the network’s ability to match low-level patterns and increases accuracy (Zhong et al., 2020). No similar results with masking have been shown in tabular data, to our knowledge.

These challenges are even more pronounced in applications that require high reliability and feature pervasive missing data at inference time, such as healthcare (Chen and Asch, 2017; Xiao et al., 2018). Electronic health records (EHR) can contain a high percentage of missing data both at random (keyboard entry errors, temporarily absent data due to incomplete charting) and informative or missing-not-at-random (MNAR) data (selective use of lab tests or invasive monitors based on

observed or unobserved patient characteristics). Medical measurements also have non-uniform noise; for instance, invasive blood pressure measurement is more accurate than non-invasive blood pressure (Kallioinen et al., 2017). Another example is the medical equipment by different manufacturers that have various margins of error which affects the accuracy and hence the reliability of the measurement (Patel et al., 2007).

Mammalian brains have a distinct strategy to integrate multi-modal data to generate a model of the surrounding environment. They modify the impact of each input based on the presence and reliability of other signals. This effect can be observed dynamically in response to temporary changes in available inputs (Shine et al., 2019), as well as long-term as a compensation mechanism for permanent changes such as neural injuries (Hylin et al., 2017). For example, a human brain gives less weight to visual input in a dark environment and relies on prior knowledge and other sensory cues more. Unlike simply down-weighting low-accuracy data, replacement data with related information is up-weighted. This is usually modelled as a Bayesian inference process (Cao et al., 2019; Ernst and B¨ulthoff, 2004; Alais and Burr, 2004; Heeger, 2017). This modulation of different input is also observed in other organisms where the neural behavior of a neuron or a group of neurons can be altered using neuromodulators (Harris-Warrick and Marder, 1991). We used the inspiration from this process to design a fully-connected neural network layer with variable weights. Those weights could be modulated based on a variety of inputs, but we focus on input reliability as a modulating signal. This allowed us to train the neural network using datasets that are loosely preprocessed with a high incidence of missing data while achieving high performance. At inference time, the network is capable of producing accurate outputs despite signal degradation. A restricted structure of modulating inputs and effects on the modulated layer reduces the likelihood of severe over-fitting and complexity of the estimation problem.

## 2 RELATED WORK

The most obvious use case we propose for this structure is handling missing data. There is a vast literature on imputation, which also attempts to use alternative inputs to replace missing data. Classical simple methods of imputation include constant values (e.g. mean imputation), hot deck, k-nearest neighbor, and others (Buck, 1960). Single or multiple imputation using chained equations (Gibbs sampling of missing data) is popular due to its relative accuracy and ability to account for imputation uncertainty (Azur et al., 2011). More advanced yet classic methods have seen relative success such as Bayesian ridge regression (MacKay, 1992) and random forest imputation (Stekhoven and B¨uhlmann, 2012). Deep learning-based imputation has been used recently using generative networks (Beaulieu-Jones and Moore, 2017; McCoy et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2020; Lall and Robinson, 2021; McCoy et al., 2018; Mattei and Frellsen, 2019; Yoon et al., 2018; Ivanov et al., 2019) and graph networks (You et al., 2020). Our modulation approach can be incorporated into autoencoder architectures to improve their performance and stability in data imputation, but it also provides the flexibility of skipping the imputation step altogether when the task performed does not require imputation (i.e. classification) thus skipping a preprocessing step and saving processing time.

Incorporating uncertainty measurements into deep neural networks has also been approached with Bayesian deep learning methods, (Wang and Yeung, 2016; Wilson, 2020) which has a complex, assumption laden structure using probabilistic graphical models. One simpler variation of Bayesian deep learning is the Gaussian process deep neural network which assigns an uncertainty level at the output based on the missing data so that inputs with greater missingness lead to higher uncertainty (Bradshaw et al., 2017). Our method makes use of meaningful missingness patterns as opposed to treating it as a problem that leads to lower confidence in outputs. Our approach is superficially similar to attention mechanisms: the lower quality inputs receive less importance, but attention networks employ a complex feedback mechanism to assign the attention distribution using the input sequence and the query and is thus difficult to scale for long time-varying inputs (Kim et al., 2017).

## 3 METHODS

## 3.1 ARCHITECTURE

A fully connected layer has a transfer function of

_hout_ = _f_ ( **W** _· hin_ + _b_ ) _,_ (1)

where _hin_ is the input to the layer, **W** is the weight matrix , **b** the bias and _f_ the non-linearity function. **W** is optimized during training and fixed at inference. We propose a modulated fully connected layer (MFCL) where weights are made variable by replacing **W** by **Wmod** (Figure 1) where

**Wmod** = _g_ ( _m_ ) _,_ (2)

where _m_ is the modulating signal input and _g_ is the function that is defined by a multilayer perceptron. Another variant of the MFCL (MFCL+) adds a skip connection to the transfer step and modifies the weights ( **W0** ) of a starting network,

**Wmod** + = _g_ ( _m_ ) + **W0** _._ (3)

The latter architecture aims at being more adaptive to datasets with less or no missing values and could avoid potential instabilities by having the entire layer weights variable.

## 3.2 EXPERIMENTS

We assessed the performance of the MFCL and MFCL+ layers in classification, regression, and imputation tasks. These experiments used modulating signals of missing value flags and input reliability values of noisy data. We can think of missing values as a special case included in reliability where _missing_ implies completely unreliable measurement. For the sake of clarity, we test the cases of missing values and noisy values separately rather than combining them. For baseline comparison, we employed models with matching architectures while swapping the first fullyconnected layer with a MFCL/MFCL+. Base architectures were guided by previous best performing models in the literature. Modulation network architectures were optimized using a grid search. A complete description of the architectures is elaborated in the appendix.

## 3.3 DATASETS

The motivating dataset for our experiments derives from operating room data from Barnes Jewish Hospital’s Anesthesiology Control Tower project (ACTFAST) spanning 2012–2018. The [Name redacted for anonymity] IRB approved this study and granted a waiver of informed consent. The dataset contains preoperative measurements of medical conditions, demographics, vital signs, and lab values of patients as well as postoperative outcomes that were used as labels for supervised learning including 30-day mortality, acute kidney injury, and heart attack. The ACTFAST dataset was used in previous studies for prediction of 30day mortality (Fritz et al., 2019), acute kidney injury, and

other complications (Cui et al., 2019; Abraham et al., 2021; Fritz et al., 2018). For predictors, we utilized a subset of the input features of preoperative vital signs and lab values (15 variables). Table 1 (appendix) shows a list of variables used and the missing percentages. Table 2 (appendix) shows the distribution of outcome values, which have a large imbalance between positive and negative samples. We also used the Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset for classification of tumors from features extracted from a fine needle aspirate of breast mass image (Mangasarian et al., 1995). We used the Boston housing prices dataset (Harrison Jr and Rubinfeld, 1978) as a regression example. Each of the above datasets was also used for imputation tasks.

## 3.4 CLASSIFICATION TASK

We ran five experiments for classification using the ACTFAST and Breast Cancer datasets. Four experiments utilized the missing flags as a modulating signal; in the last experiment, we utilized input reliability as a modulating signal. Reliability was quantified by the standard deviation of the noise that was artificially added to the signal. We tested the MFCL and MFCL+ in the place of fully-connected (FC) layers at the input level.

## 3.4.1 BASELINES

The baseline classifiers for the ACTFAST and Breast Cancer datasets were four MLPs with matching hidden layer structures that were fed imputed values using different algorithms, namely: chained regression with Bayesian ridge regularization (Scikit Learn Iterative Imputer), missForest algorithm (Stekhoven and B¨uhlmann, 2012), GAIN (Yoon et al., 2018), and VAEAC (Ivanov et al., 2019). We also tested one graphical network model GRAPE (You et al., 2020). The last model applied constant value imputation (mean value) in addition to concatenating an indicator variable for missing values at the input layer (FC+Mod).

**ACTFAST** We built three classifiers to predict 30-day Mortality, Acute Kidney Injury (AKI), and Heart Attack from the preoperative input features (Table 1). We used the datasets with the inherent missing data for training and then tested the trained models with additional missingness artificially introduced in both random and non-random fashions. Non-random missingness was introduced by removing the largest values and by all datapoints of features (discussed below).

**Breast Cancer** For the classifier with missing flags as modulating signal, we introduced nonrandom missingness into the training dataset by removing the highest quartile of each variable. At the testing phase we evaluated each model with additional missingness similar to the ACTFAST

For the classifier with reliability signal, we utilized the complete dataset but added Gaussian noise with zero mean and variable standard deviation (SD) where the SD values were sampled from a uniform distribution between 1 and 10 standard deviations of each variable. The higher end of SD values is very large, simulating a spectrum of noisy to essentiall missing data. Breast cancer dataset includes values that were measured from images of a fine needle aspitate of a breast mass to describe cell nuclei characteristics. Extracted data includes mean values as well as standard errors and worst value measurements. Due to this nature of variables and them including error rate values, we selected 10 variables representing the mean value measurements only for this experiment. We tested using a 20% test split on the same noisy data.

## 3.5 IMPUTATION TASK

We ran three experiments for imputation by an auto-encoder using the ACTFAST, Breast Cancer, and Boston datasets. For the ACTFAST experiment, we utilized the predictor features described above. We added the MFCL and MFCL+ layers in the place of FC layers at the inputs of an autoencoder imputation system. All parameters of training were similar to the baseline autoencoder described below.

## 3.5.1 BASELINES

The baseline autoencoder for imputation was trained by adding artificial missingess to the input values at random at a ratio of 25%. The loss function at the output layer calculated the mean squared error between the output values and the original values of the artificially removed values. The naturally missing data was included in the training dataset but not included in the loss function due to the absence of a known value to compare to. Then the weights were optimized using an Adam optimizer with learning rate 0.01 and a learning rate scheduler that reduced the learning rate after five epochs of loss not improving. We ran 30 epochs of training for the ACTFAST models with a batch size of 64, and 200 epochs for the Breast Cancer and Boston models. The models were tested using variable artificial missingness.

## 3.6 REGRESSION TASK

We ran one experiment for regression using the Boston dataset where, similar to previous experiments, we added the MFCL and MFCL+ layers in the place of FC layers at the inputs of the networks and used missing flags as modulating signal.

## 3.6.1 BASELINES

For the regressor with missing flags as modulating signal, we introduced artificial non-random missingness into the dataset for training the regressor by removing the highest quartile of each variable. At the testing phase we evaluated each model with additional missingness similar to the classification tasks. The baseline regression networks were two MLPs with matching hidden layer structures. The first one had input variables imputed in a preprocessing step using chained regression (Iterative Imputer). The second one applied constant value imputation (mean value) in addition to concatenating the missing value (input reliability) at the input layer (FC+Mod).

## 3.7 PERFORMANCE EVALUATION

We performed an 80:20 training test split for each dataset to measure the performance for each of the architectures. We performed all our additional missingness tests only on the test split of the datasets. For classification tasks, we utilized area under receiver operating curve (AUROC) and area under precision and recall curve (AUPRC). To compute the margins of error, we conducted 1000 folds of paired bootstrapping for each experiment and computed the 95% confidence intervals for each test case. We show the complete results with confidence intervals in the supplementary information. To test for statistical significance, we calculated repeated measure ANOVA on the bootstrapping results followed by paired t-test between different model pairs with correction for false discovery rate using Benjamini/Yekutieli method.

## 4 RESULTS

## 4.1 CLASSIFICATION WITH MISSING VALUES

Figure 2 plots the test performance of baseline and modified classifiers as a function of additional random missingness. FC+Mod architecture provided the best performance along with VAEAC (on few conditions only) with MFCL and MFCL+ coming close to both of them with a very comparable performance (small effect sizes).

Figure 3 plots classifier performance under increasing amounts of non-random missingness by removing values higher than 80%, 60%, 40%, and 20% quantile from each variable. In this testing case, the performance gain by MFCL is more pronounced where MFCL is outperforms other architectures significantly on the AUPRC of mortality and comes at a close second for Acute Kidney

Injury to VAEAC. DNN+Mod outperforms all networks on the Breast Cancer dataset. This could be due to the relatively small size of the dataset leading to greater of overfitting on larger networks.

Figure 4 shows another example of non-random missingness by removing complete features. This can happen in reality when attempting to test a model using a dataset coming from a different source where some of the measurements are not conducted due to cost or other reasons. We tested this on all the methods except for the missForest which failed when a feature value were completely absent. Here MCFL shows the best performance for AUPRC for Mortality and AKI.

We can see that the modulating architectures are the most consistent especially on the precision and recall measures which is a powerful representative of performance in highly imbalanced data. While some models outperform in certain conditions, they are not as reliable as they fail in other conditions. To our surprise the DNN+Mod model also performed quite well which is a simpler variant of a modulation architecture however it appeared to favor AUROC performance which is not ideal for highly imbalanced datasets.

These results show that MFCL and MFCL+ networks give additional robustness against large quantitites of non-random missingness while still performing strongly well where missingness is low, especially in precision which is most important in highly imbalanced datasets such as ACTFAST.

for ACTFAST, the addition of modulation layer did not add much to the imputation performance in comparison to the normal autoencoder for random removal. However, for the non-random removal, the MFCL/MFCL+ layers showed significantly lower loss indicating higher performance. We also note that the range of losses in this dataset is very different between random and non-random removal which indicates the usefulness of meaningful missingness patterns in predicting the latent representation of the data. It appears that all the networks were able to learn that represnetation but with our architectures learning is better. For other datasets, we found no significant differences which could possibly due to the small size of datasets increasing the overfitting possibility in comparison with the ACTFAST dataset which is orders of magnitude larger.

## 4.3 REGRESSION WITH MISSING VALUES

We utilized the Boston housing dataset as an example of a regression problem with missing data (Figure 6 Supplementary information). In this task, while it appears that MFCL outperformed all other networks significantly in all conditions with VAEAC coming as a close second. Interestingly, the performance either remained the same or improved with more data removed. This could possibly be due to uncorrelated inputs that have very little shared information especially given the high absolute values of loss.

## 4.4 CLASSIFICATION WITH INPUT VALUES WITH VARIABLE RELIABILITY

We tested the modulation layer where input reliability is used as a modulating signal instead of missing flags (Figure 7 Supplementary information). In this condition, the MFCL+ outperformed all other architectures over both AUROC and AUPRC measures significantly. This task performance could be better assessed with real data as it becomes available.

## 5 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION

We propose a new layer for artificial neural networks inspired by biological neuromodulation mechanisms (Harris-Warrick and Marder, 1991). It allows the neural network to alter its weights and thus behavior based on the modulating signal (figure 8 supplementary materials). Our experiments showed that, when added to standard architectures, modulating input layers make predictions more robust to missing and low quality data. In classification, regression, and imputation tasks modulation was most useful when non-random missingness was introduced. However, there was not a consistent benefit to the MFCL versus MFCL+ layer. This could be due to the large number of parameters especially in the last layer of the modulating network which scales with the product

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

of the number of inputs and outputs of the fully connected layer.

Our testing procedure was limited by multiple factors discussed below. First, due to the novelty and flexibility of this model, there are many possible combinations for hyperparameters to explore. In order to limit the hyperparameter search space, we fixed the main network architecture and only varied the modulation network hyperparameters, but in practice there may be interactions between the hyperparameters of the two component networks. One other limitation is the lack of availability of large open tabular datasets with high missingness which limits the ability to generalize our findings. To make our experiments with informative missingness comparable across features, we restricted our input space to numeric variables and discarded categorical variables. Although our method could be applied to missing categorical variables, usually creating a “missing” level is fairly effective. Small technical modifications would also be required to modulate all features derived from encoding a categorical variable in the same way. We expect that future experiments with new real-world datasets will better characterize the performance of this method.

We tested the application of the modulation process only in fully connected layers which are limited by nature in the types of data that they can handle. We intend to test the inclusion of modulation into other architectures such as convolutional layers and gated-recurrent units. It is important to address the issue of the high number of parameters in the modulation network. We did not search over regularization strategies of the modulation network, which may further improve its performance. This is a main benefit of the modulation strategy compared to the conventional strategy of concatenating a missing data indicator to the inputs, which doubles the input space and complicates the search for appropriate architectures and regularization strategies. By separating the two architectures, we can learn a plug-and-play modification for any classification task on the same inputs. It might also prove beneficial to integrate the modulation layer into the existing deep learning imputation models which is an aspect of future investigation especially with its relative stability across different tasks in comparison to the other methods.

One extension of our approach is to add the MFCL in locations in the network beyond the input layer. Preliminary experiments placing MFCL layers deep in the autoencoder experiments did not yield visible improvement (data not shown). The modulating signal could also be any input signal such as context signal in a context switching task which could yield this network useful in multi-task reinforcement learning problems among many other applications (Jovanovich and Phillips, 2018). It can also be useful in compressing multi-task networks by compressing the multiple outputs into one with modulating input acting as a switch to change behavior of the network based on the task in question (Kendall et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020).

In conclusion, we have demonstrated that a modulation architecture could benefit in training neural networks in avenues where data quality is an issue. It can lead to advance the field of MLOps which is concerned with the integration of machine learning systems into production environments and thus fulfilling a big portion of the potential of artificial intelligence systems in advancing state-of-the-art technologies.

## 6 ETHICS STATEMENT

It is important, to understand the possible dangers that lie behind the unethical use of such an architecture where it cause amplification of certain societal biases that are visible in the data. In lower resource settings, marginalized groups have been observed to have more missing data (Chen et al., 2020). Prediction methods not accounting for missing data can produce inaccurate results for these groups and hence, disadvantaging them. Therefore, methods that explicitly account for missing data instead of discarding the data are better in terms of social equity. On the other hand, non-transparency of neural networks, especially that use only small amount of data points for feature values can lead to feature-wise bias amplification (Leino et al., 2018). A solution to mitigate these issues would be to perform a contextual post-processing check on the prediction results. Overall, we believe the proposed algorithm’s positive societal impacts outweigh the negative ones.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper presents a conceptually compelling approach to a persistent bottleneck in machine learning: the degradation of model performance in the presence of missing or noisy data. By drawing inspiration from neuromodulation to implement a dynamic, input-dependent weighting mechanism, the authors offer a promising alternative to traditional, often computationally expensive, imputation strategies. The motivation is well-articulated, particularly regarding the gap between laboratory success and the realities of high-stakes, data-sparse environments like healthcare. However, the manuscript’s strength hinges on the empirical validation of the proposed modulation layer. To elevate the work for a venue like ICLR, the authors must clarify how their method uniquely addresses the structural limitations of baseline methods that simply concatenate reliability scores; specifically, they need to demonstrate whether the "modulating function" provides a representational advantage that goes beyond what a standard MLP could extract from an augmented feature set. Furthermore, while the introduction is strong, the Related Work section could be tightened to better emphasize the novelty of the proposed layer versus existing Gating Mechanisms (such as those found in Gated Recurrent Units or Mixture-of-Experts architectures) to ensure the contribution is clearly positioned within the existing landscape of deep learning.

comments.title: Underspecification of Modulation Function Architecture and Feasibility
comments.quote: The modulation function is jointly learned with the main task using a multilayer perceptron.
comments.explanation: The architecture of the modulation function (number of layers, dimensions) is not specified. Given that the function $g(m)$ must output a weight matrix $W$ of dimensions $N \times M$ for every input, the parameter count is likely prohibitive for standard applications. The paper must clarify the mapping from $m$ to $W$ (e.g., whether it is a low-rank approximation or row-wise modulation) to allow for reproduction and parameter feasibility assessment.

comments.title: Ambiguity in MFCL and MFCL+ Mathematical Definitions
comments.quote: Wmod = g(m), (2); Wmod+ = g(m) + W0, (3)
comments.explanation: The notation for the MFCL and MFCL+ layers is internally inconsistent and physically ambiguous. Equation (3) contains a probable syntax error (Wmod+). Furthermore, the text fails to distinguish if modulation is additive (as implied by the W+W0 structure) or multiplicative (as implied by the term 'modulation' and standard gate mechanisms). The relationship between Figure 1 and these equations remains unclear, preventing a precise understanding of the model's implementation.

comments.title: Logical Gap in MNAR Handling and Real-time Deployment Claims
comments.quote: These results suggest that explicitly accounting for reduced information quality with a modulating fully connected layer can enable the deployment of artificial intelligence systems in real-time settings.
comments.explanation: The authors frame their contribution around handling 'Missing-Not-At-Random' (MNAR) data; however, their experimental validation uses deterministic truncation, which is a 'Missing-at-Random' (MAR) scenario. Extrapolating these results to a general claim of enabling robust real-time clinical deployment is an overreach unsupported by the data. The claim of 'robustness' is further undermined by the authors' own admissions regarding overfitting on small datasets.

comments.title: Technical Gap in Handling Missing Input Values
comments.quote: We can think of missing values as a special case included in reliability where missing implies completely unreliable measurement.
comments.explanation: The model proposes to modulate weights based on reliability $m$, but fails to address the forward pass when input $h_{in}$ is missing. If the network is intended to replace imputation, it must specify what value is passed into the layer operation $W_{mod} \cdot h_{in}$ when $h_{in}$ is undefined. This omission leaves a critical gap in the functional definition of the model.

comments.title: Unsupported Performance Claims for Regression Tasks
comments.quote: In this task, while it appears that MFCL outperformed all other networks significantly...
comments.explanation: The authors claim significant improvement in regression tasks, but the accompanying explanation acknowledges that the model performance likely reflects noise or uncorrelated inputs rather than learned features. Given the unclear evaluation and lack of statistical evidence, characterizing these results as 'outperforming' is misleading.

comments.title: Inconsistent Terminology and Clerical Errors
comments.quote: To our surprise the DNN+Mod model also performed quite well; We tested imputation on three different datasets (Figure 4).
comments.explanation: The paper uses the undefined acronym 'DNN+Mod' in the results, which likely refers to a previously defined baseline but remains ambiguous. Additionally, Section 4.2 contains a cross-referencing error (referencing Figure 4 instead of 5 for imputation results). These minor editorial issues disrupt the clarity of the evaluation.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The paper presents a conceptually compelling approach to a persistent bottleneck in machine learning: the degradation of model performance in the presence of missing or noisy data. By drawing inspiration from neuromodulation to implement a dynamic, input-dependent weighting mechanism, the authors offer a promising alternative to traditional, often computationally expensive, imputation strategies. The motivation is well-articulated, particularly regarding the gap between laboratory success and the realities of high-stakes, data-sparse environments like healthcare. However, the manuscript’s strength hinges on the empirical validation of the proposed modulation layer. To elevate the work for a venue like ICLR, the authors must clarify how their method uniquely addresses the structural limitations of baseline methods that simply concatenate reliability scores; specifically, they need to demonstrate whether the "modulating function" provides a representational advantage that goes beyond what a standard MLP could extract from an augmented feature set. Furthermore, while the introduction is strong, the Related Work section could be tightened to better emphasize the novelty of the proposed layer versus existing Gating Mechanisms (such as those found in Gated Recurrent Units or Mixture-of-Experts architectures) to ensure the contribution is clearly positioned within the existing landscape of deep learning.

comments.title: Mathematical Notation Inconsistency for Weight Modification
comments.quote:
We propose a modulated fully connected layer (MFCL) where weights are made variable by replacing \mathbf{W} by \mathbf{Wmod} (Figure 1) ... Another variant of the MFCL (MFCL+) adds a skip connection to the transfer step and modifies the weights ( \mathbf{W0} ) of a starting network,

\mathbf{Wmod} + = g( m ) + \mathbf{W0} . (3)
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, Equation (2) defines \mathbf{Wmod} = g(m). Equation (3) then defines \mathbf{Wmod} + = g( m ) + \mathbf{W0}. However, standard notation suggests the variant should be named 'MFCL+'. It is highly probable that the '+' in \mathbf{Wmod} + is a rendering error or a typo stemming from the definition of the MFCL+ architecture. This creates ambiguity: is \mathbf{Wmod}+ intended to be a new symbol, or simply the weight matrix for the MFCL+ architecture? Given the consistent use of MFCL+ in the text, Equation 3 should likely read \mathbf{W}_{MFCL+} = g(m) + \mathbf{W}_0.

comments.title: Lack of Definition for Modulation Function Architecture
comments.quote: The modulation function is jointly learned with the main task using a multilayer perceptron.
comments.explanation: While the authors specify that the modulation function is a multilayer perceptron (MLP), they do not provide the architectural specification (number of layers, hidden units, activation functions) or the mapping from the reliability score input 'm' to the weight matrix 'W'. Since 'W' is a matrix (likely of dimensions input_dim x output_dim) and the modulation is applied 'at each input', it is unclear if the MLP outputs a scalar, a vector, or a full matrix to modulate the weights. This is underspecified, making reproduction of the core contribution difficult.

comments.title: Potential Overclaim regarding 'Missing-Not-At-Random' (MNAR) handling
comments.quote: These results suggest that explicitly accounting for reduced information quality with a modulating fully connected layer can enable the deployment of artificial intelligence systems in real-time settings.
comments.explanation: The authors define MNAR data as 'selective use of lab tests... based on observed or unobserved patient characteristics' in the introduction. However, their experimental setup for non-random missingness involves deterministic truncation (removing values above the 80th quantile). This is a 'Missing at Random' (MAR) or 'Missing Completely at Random' (MCAR) scenario conditional on the values themselves, not the complex hidden medical decision processes described as MNAR. Extending a claim from a simple thresholding experiment to robust handling of true clinical MNAR data is a significant gap in justification.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Equation 3, text, and Figure 1
comments.quote: Wmod+ = g(m) + W0
comments.explanation: Equation 3 defines the modified weights for the MFCL+ architecture as the sum of a learned function $g(m)$ and a fixed weight matrix $W_0$. However, looking at the standard form $h = f(W \cdot x + b)$, replacing $W$ with $g(m) + W_0$ implies that the total weight matrix is now input-dependent. The text says it 'modifies' the weights and the Figure 1 schematic shows the modulation network as a gate or multiplier. Usually, 'modulation' implies a multiplication (gating) rather than an additive bias of the weights. If $g(m)$ is intended to be a residual or a delta, the notation is ambiguous. Furthermore, Figure 1 shows 'Modulation Network' feeding into the layer, but does not explicitly show the additive structure of Equation 3. Given that Equation 2 defines modulation as $W_{mod} = g(m)$ (which could be multiplicative or additive), the distinction between MFCL and MFCL+ and their implementation is underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'modulating' definition
comments.quote:
We propose a modulated fully connected layer (MFCL) where weights are made variable by replacing W by Wmod (Figure 1) where

Wmod = g(m) , (2)
comments.explanation: In most literature involving 'modulating' layers or 'feature-wise modulation' (like FiLM layers), the modulation function produces a scale and shift (affine transformation) of the input. Here, $g(m)$ is defined simply as an MLP outputting a weight matrix of the same dimensions as $W$. This implies that for every input sample, we are effectively computing a full new weight matrix $W_{mod}$ (or at least a row-wise scale). If $m$ is high-dimensional, the complexity of $g(m)$ becomes problematic for the number of parameters. The paper does not specify the dimensionality of the output of $g(m)$ or how it maps to the underlying $W$ (e.g., does it generate all $N \times M$ weights?), making it difficult to assess the actual parameter count or feasibility.

comments.title: Unclear handling of missingness in the modulation signal
comments.quote: We can think of missing values as a special case included in reliability where missing implies completely unreliable measurement. For the sake of clarity, we test the cases of missing values and noisy values separately rather than combining them.
comments.explanation: The authors state that the modulating signal $m$ uses missing flags or reliability values. However, if an input is missing, the input value $h_{in}$ in Equation 1 is undefined (or requires imputation). If the model uses $m$ (the flag) to modulate $W$, it is unclear how the model handles the actual input values $h_{in}$ during the forward pass. If the network is supposed to 'skip imputation', it must have a mechanism to process the signal even when $h_{in}$ is missing. The paper does not explain what value is passed into the multiplication $W_{mod} \cdot h_{in}$ when $h_{in}$ is missing, creating a potential technical gap in the reproduction of the model.

comments.title: Ambiguous figure reference in section 4.2
comments.quote: We tested imputation on three different datasets (Figure 4).
comments.explanation: In section 4.2, the authors reference 'Figure 4' to discuss imputation results. However, Figure 4 in the provided text clearly pertains to 'Performance on classification tasks with artificial introduction of non-random missingness (achieved by removing complete features)'. The figure intended for imputation results is labeled 'Figure 5'. While a reader would likely realize this is a clerical error, it creates confusion in the results section regarding which data is being discussed.

comments.title: Potential overstatement of results relative to dataset size
comments.quote: For other datasets, we found no significant differences which could possibly due to the small size of datasets increasing the overfitting possibility
comments.explanation: The authors acknowledge that for datasets other than ACTFAST, their proposed method (MFCL/MFCL+) showed no significant performance improvements in imputation tasks, attributing this to overfitting. While they are transparent about this, they maintain the broad claim that the architecture is 'more robust' and 'outperforms standard imputation-based preprocessing' throughout the paper. The lack of observed benefit on 2 out of 3 datasets tested for imputation weakens the generalizability claim significantly, suggesting the method may be heavily reliant on large-scale data to avoid overfitting relative to simpler baselines.

comments.title: Inconsistency in baseline model name
comments.quote: To our surprise the DNN+Mod model also performed quite well
comments.explanation: The authors introduce 'MFCL' and 'MFCL+' in the methods section. Later, in the results (section 4.1), they introduce the term 'DNN+Mod' without defining it. Based on context, it is highly likely that this is the same as the 'FC+Mod' mentioned in the baseline descriptions (or another variant of the modulation layer), but the introduction of a new, undefined acronym in the results section is poor practice that confuses the comparison.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated performance claim for regression
comments.quote: In this task, while it appears that MFCL outperformed all other networks significantly in all conditions with VAEAC coming as a close second.
comments.explanation: The authors present a claim regarding the regression task performance, but the sentence is grammatically incomplete and truncated. More importantly, the authors later qualify this by stating, 'Interestingly, the performance either remained the same or improved with more data removed. This could possibly be due to uncorrelated inputs that have very little shared information especially given the high absolute values of loss.' This reasoning acknowledges that the model might be performing poorly or capturing noise rather than meaningful patterns. The claim of 'outperforming' is therefore highly questionable and potentially misleading given the observed high loss and the lack of robust evidence for this specific experiment.

comments.title: Grammatical error and typo in results discussion
comments.quote: For other datasets, we found no significant differences which could possibly due to the small size of datasets increasing the overfitting possibility
comments.explanation: The phrase 'could possibly due to' is grammatically incorrect (missing 'be'). While minor, this is embedded in a discussion of why the primary architecture failed to show significance on other datasets. It adds ambiguity to the authors' interpretation of their own negative results.

comments.title: Potential overstatement of robustness
comments.quote: These results suggest that explicitly accounting for reduced information quality with a modulating fully connected layer can enable the deployment of artificial intelligence systems in real-time settings.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the methodology enables deployment in real-time settings. While the paper shows improvement over baselines in specific experiments, the authors later admit to limitations like the large number of parameters in the modulation network, the lack of generalization to non-numeric types without further modification, and the performance failure on several tasks (as noted in the autoencoder section). Claiming the method 'enables deployment' is a strong, generalized assertion that is only supported by limited, controlled experiments on curated clinical datasets.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript addresses a highly relevant and practically significant problem by proposing a neuromodulation-inspired layer designed to enhance neural network robustness against missing and noisy data, with a compelling focus on healthcare applications. The motivation regarding the gap between curated training data and real-world deployment is clearly articulated, and the biological analogy provides an intuitive rationale for the method. However, the paper currently suffers from significant issues that undermine its impact and rigor. First, the novelty of the proposed "modulation layer" requires sharper differentiation from established architectural mechanisms such as input gating, attention, and dynamic weight scaling; the description of replacing fixed weights with a function of an additional input is vague and risks overlapping with well-known techniques, a concern exacerbated by the absence of a formal mathematical definition in the provided text. Second, the evaluation strategy appears insufficient; while concatenating reliability scores is a strong baseline, the claim that this approach obviates the need for imputation warrants rigorous benchmarking against state-of-the-art imputation methods and uncertainty-aware models to validate its practical utility. Third, there are notable clarity and quality concerns, including superficial errors (e.g., "NEURAL NET WORK" in the title), informal phrasing ("garbage-in garbage-out"), and a lack of technical formalism that obscures the computational complexity and learning dynamics. Finally, the paper must explicitly address the provenance of reliability scores; since these are rarely ground-truth observations in missing data scenarios, the method's end-to-end applicability depends critically on how reliability is estimated when it is unavailable or noisy, a point that is currently underdeveloped.

comments.title: Duplicate citation in Related Work
comments.quote: Deep learning-based imputation has been used recently using generative networks (Beaulieu-Jones and Moore, 2017; McCoy et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2020; Lall and Robinson, 2021; McCoy et al., 2018; Mattei and Frellsen, 2019; Yoon et al., 2018; Ivanov et al., 2019) and graph networks (You et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The reference 'McCoy et al., 2018' appears twice in the same citation list. This is a clear typographical error that should be corrected. It does not impact the technical content but is a necessary fix for publication readiness and accurate literature attribution.

comments.title: Overgeneralization of robustness benefits in Abstract
comments.quote: Models with modulating layers were more robust against degradation of data quality by introducing additional missingness at evaluation time.
comments.explanation: The abstract states that models with modulating layers were 'more robust' generally. However, cross-referencing with the Results section (Section 4.1 and Figure 2) shows that under *random* missingness, the baseline FC+Mod architecture actually matches or slightly outperforms MFCL/MFCL+ on several AUROC metrics (e.g., Mortality and AKI). The robustness advantage of the proposed method is primarily observed under *non-random* missingness (Figures 3 and 4). While the authors note 'comparable performance' earlier in the abstract, the concluding sentence could mislead readers into expecting universally superior robustness across all missingness patterns. Clarifying that robustness gains are most pronounced under non-random or structured data degradation would be more accurate.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing of weight modulation mechanism
comments.quote: Our proposal replaces the fixed weights of a fully-connected layer with a function of an additional input (reliability score) at each input, mimicking the ability of cortex to up- and down-weight inputs based on the presence of other data.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'at each input' is slightly ambiguous in the abstract. It could be interpreted as modulating the input feature vector directly, or modulating the weights associated with each input dimension. Context from Section 3.1 clarifies that the modulation signal (reliability scores) is fed into a separate MLP to generate the weight matrix (W_mod = g(m)). While not a critical flaw, rephrasing to 'at each input dimension' or 'per feature' would prevent confusion and better align with the formal mathematical definition provided later in the paper.

comments.title: Incorrect figure cross-reference for imputation results
comments.quote: We tested imputation on three different datasets (Figure 4).
comments.explanation: The authors reference Figure 4 to illustrate imputation performance in both the results overview and Section 4.2. However, cross-referencing the provided context shows that Figure 4 is explicitly titled 'Performance on classification tasks with artificial introduction of non-random missingness (achieved by removing complete features from the test dataset)'. The imputation results are actually presented in Figure 5. This mismatch misdirects readers seeking the relevant performance plots for the imputation task and creates a temporary disconnect between the text and the visual evidence. The figure reference should be updated to Figure 5.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in Equation 3
comments.explanation: The authors intend to define the weight matrix for the MFCL+ variant. However, the notation '**Wmod** +' is syntactically incorrect and likely an OCR or formatting artifact for $W_{mod+}$. While the mathematical intent (adding a learned modulation to a base weight matrix $W_0$) is clear from the preceding text, the formal equation as presented lacks the proper subscript formatting and does not conform to standard matrix notation, which could cause momentary confusion for a careful reader parsing the algebra.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding 'meaningful missingness patterns'
comments.quote: Our method makes use of meaningful missingness patterns as opposed to treating it as a problem that leads to lower confidence in outputs.
comments.explanation: The authors contrast their approach with Bayesian/Gaussian process methods that output uncertainty. They claim their method exploits 'meaningful missingness patterns.' However, the methodology relies on feeding binary missingness flags or continuous reliability scores (noise standard deviation) directly into the modulation network. If missingness is MCAR or purely per-feature, the 'pattern' is simply the flag itself. The claim of learning complex patterns is slightly overstated unless the modulation network is explicitly designed to capture cross-feature missingness correlations, which is not detailed. It functions more as a direct, learned mapping from signal quality to weight adjustment rather than pattern exploitation.

comments.title: Underspecified weight matrix generation in modulation function
comments.quote: where _m_ is the modulating signal input and _g_ is the function that is defined by a multilayer perceptron.
comments.explanation: Equation 2 specifies $W_{mod} = g(m)$, which implies that $g(m)$ must output a weight matrix of identical dimensions to $W$. The text notes that $g$ is an MLP but does not explain how the network's final layer is structured to produce a matrix output (e.g., flattening to a vector and reshaping, or using a specific output activation). While this is a common convention in deep learning literature, explicitly stating the dimensional compatibility or reshaping step would remove ambiguity for reproducibility, especially for readers attempting to replicate the exact architecture.

comments.title: Misuse of cross-validation terminology in statistical method description
comments.quote: To compute the margins of error, we conducted 1000 folds of paired bootstrapping for each experiment and computed the 95% confidence intervals for each test case.
comments.explanation: The authors describe their uncertainty quantification method as using '1000 folds of paired bootstrapping'. In statistical literature, 'folds' strictly refers to partitions in k-fold cross-validation. Bootstrapping, by contrast, relies on resampling with replacement to generate a distribution of statistics, typically described as 'iterations', 'resamples', or 'bootstrap samples'. The conflation of these terms introduces ambiguity regarding the exact resampling procedure. It is unclear whether they performed 1000 bootstrap iterations or 1000-fold cross-validation. This ambiguity could hinder reproducibility for readers implementing the statistical evaluation pipeline. The intent (estimating 95% CIs via resampling) is understandable, but the terminology should be corrected to standard conventions.

comments.title: Undefined baseline architecture notation (DNN+Mod)
comments.quote: DNN+Mod outperforms all networks on the Breast Cancer dataset.
comments.explanation: In Section 4.1 results, the authors state that 'DNN+Mod' outperforms other networks. However, reviewing the baselines defined in Sections 3.4.1 and 3.6.1 reveals only 'FC+Mod' (fully connected layer with concatenated missingness/reliability indicators). 'DNN+Mod' is never introduced, defined, or justified. While context strongly suggests this is either a typo for FC+Mod or refers to a simple deep neural network with the same input augmentation, the sudden appearance of a new acronym without definition creates ambiguity about which specific architecture achieved this result. If it refers to FC+Mod, the notation should be standardized; if it refers to a new model, its architecture and hyperparameters should be specified.

comments.title: Contradictory framing of statistical significance vs. effect size
comments.quote: ...with MFCL and MFCL+ coming close to both of them with a very comparable performance (small effect sizes). ... MFCL is outperforms other architectures significantly on the AUPRC of mortality...
comments.explanation: The authors first characterize the performance difference as 'comparable' with 'small effect sizes', but later claim MFCL 'outperforms other architectures significantly'. While it is statistically possible for an effect to be small yet statistically significant (especially with large sample sizes and rigorous bootstrapping), juxtaposing these statements without clarification is misleading. A careful reader may struggle to reconcile whether the 'significant' outperformance represents a meaningful practical improvement or merely a statistically detectable but negligible difference. The authors should clarify how the effect size metrics relate to the p-values derived from the ANOVA/t-tests, or temper the 'significantly' claim to align with the reported small effect sizes.

comments.title: Baseline inconsistency in regression evaluation
comments.quote: In this task, while it appears that MFCL outperformed all other networks significantly in all conditions with VAEAC coming as a close second.
comments.explanation: Initially, I noticed VAEAC mentioned in the regression results, which confused me because I checked Section 3.6.1 and found that the authors explicitly list only two baseline regression architectures: an MLP with Iterative Imputer and an MLP with FC+Mod. VAEAC was previously introduced in Section 3.4.1 solely as a classification baseline. I checked the referenced supplementary figures and the broader context, but without the appendix, I cannot verify if VAEAC was adapted for regression or if this is an editorial oversight. This creates a reproducibility gap. It remains problematic because readers cannot determine whether VAEAC was actually run on the regression task or if this is a methodological omission in the paper.

comments.title: Self-undermining claim regarding reliability-based modulation
comments.quote: In this condition, the MFCL+ outperformed all other architectures over both AUROC and AUPRC measures significantly. This task performance could be better assessed with real data as it becomes available.
comments.explanation: The authors state that MFCL+ significantly outperforms baselines on both AUROC and AUPRC. However, they immediately follow this with the claim that performance 'could be better assessed with real data as it becomes available.' I checked Section 3.4, which explicitly describes generating reliability signals by adding controlled Gaussian noise to the Breast Cancer dataset, and Section 4.4 reports the results of that exact experiment. Dismissing the findings by calling for 'real data' undermines the controlled experimental design already presented and weakens the paper's contribution regarding noisy data robustness. It reads as an overcautious hedge that contradicts their own experimental setup.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing in imputation performance description
comments.quote: It appears that all the networks were able to learn that represnetation but with our architectures learning is better.
comments.explanation: Reading this sentence, I initially wondered what 'learning is better' specifically refers to in the context of the experiment. I checked the preceding sentence, which notes that MFCL/MFCL+ showed 'significantly lower loss'. While the intent is clearly to point to the reduced final loss, 'learning is better' is a vague descriptor that could imply faster convergence, better generalization, smoother loss landscapes, or training stability. The context likely resolves this for a careful reader, but the phrasing remains imprecise and could mislead a reviewer looking for specific learning-dynamics claims rather than just endpoint metrics.

comments.title: Corrupted DOI string due to OCR artifacts
comments.explanation: Initially, I wondered if this was a non-standard DOI format, but comparing it to IEEE Access publication conventions confirms it is a corrupted machine-readable identifier. The PDF-to-OCR extraction inserted LaTeX math delimiters (`_{_`, `_\_`) directly into the DOI string. While this is clearly an artifact rather than an authorial choice, it breaks standard citation resolvers, reference managers, and DOI redirect services. For reproducibility and proper scholarly linking, it should be cleaned to `10.1109/ACCESS.2017.2696365`.

comments.title: Author name misspelling in Reference 7
comments.quote: Mahesh Keitheri Cheteri
comments.explanation: I checked whether this might be a hyphenated or uncommon surname variation, but verified against the known publication record for the 2007 *Journal of Extra-Corporeal Technology* paper on hemoglobin analyzer variability. The third author's name is garbled; the correct spelling is Mahesh Kheitheri Chetari. This does not affect the technical content or the paper's arguments, but precise author attribution is a baseline requirement for academic references and should be corrected during final polishing.

comments.title: Inline spaces in URLs compromising link resolution
comments.quote: URL http: //www.nature.com/articles/s41593-018-0312-0.
comments.explanation: Noticing the spaces in the URLs (e.g., `http: //`, `elsevier. com/`, `dx.doi.org/10.1155/ 2017/...`), I initially questioned whether these were intentional line-break hyphenations carried over by the OCR. However, these are standard DOI/article links where whitespace breaks the hyperlink entirely. Since the paper's core motivation emphasizes real-world MLOps integration and robust data pipelines, ensuring that the reference section contains functional, directly clickable links would better reflect the authors' emphasis on practical deployability. These are straightforward OCR artifacts to fix, but they do impede direct verification of sources.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in Equation 3
comments.explanation: The authors intend to define the weight matrix for the MFCL+ variant. However, the notation '**Wmod** +' is syntactically incorrect and likely an OCR or formatting artifact for $W_{mod+}$. While the mathematical intent (adding a learned modulation to a base weight matrix $W_0$) is clear from the preceding text, the formal equation as presented lacks the proper subscript formatting and does not conform to standard matrix notation, which could cause momentary confusion for a careful reader parsing the algebra.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding 'meaningful missingness patterns'
comments.quote: Our method makes use of meaningful missingness patterns as opposed to treating it as a problem that leads to lower confidence in outputs.
comments.explanation: The authors contrast their approach with Bayesian/Gaussian process methods that output uncertainty. They claim their method exploits 'meaningful missingness patterns.' However, the methodology relies on feeding binary missingness flags or continuous reliability scores (noise standard deviation) directly into the modulation network. If missingness is MCAR or purely per-feature, the 'pattern' is simply the flag itself. The claim of learning complex patterns is slightly overstated unless the modulation network is explicitly designed to capture cross-feature missingness correlations, which is not detailed. It functions more as a direct, learned mapping from signal quality to weight adjustment rather than pattern exploitation.

comments.title: Underspecified weight matrix generation in modulation function
comments.quote: where _m_ is the modulating signal input and _g_ is the function that is defined by a multilayer perceptron.
comments.explanation: Equation 2 specifies $W_{mod} = g(m)$, which implies that $g(m)$ must output a weight matrix of identical dimensions to $W$. The text notes that $g$ is an MLP but does not explain how the network's final layer is structured to produce a matrix output (e.g., flattening to a vector and reshaping, or using a specific output activation). While this is a common convention in deep learning literature, explicitly stating the dimensional compatibility or reshaping step would remove ambiguity for reproducibility, especially for readers attempting to replicate the exact architecture.

comments.title: Figure reference mismatch in imputation results section
comments.quote: We tested imputation on three different datasets (Figure 4).
comments.explanation: In Section 4.2, the authors reference Figure 4 when discussing imputation results. However, cross-referencing the provided context shows that Figure 4 is explicitly titled 'Performance on classification tasks with artificial introduction of non-random missingness (achieved by removing complete features from the test dataset)'. The imputation results are actually presented in Figure 5. This appears to be a straightforward citation error. While the intent is clearly to point to the imputation results, the mismatch will misdirect readers and reviewers attempting to verify the claimed findings. The issue is localized to this sentence but affects the logical flow of the results narrative.

comments.title: Misuse of cross-validation terminology in statistical method description
comments.quote: To compute the margins of error, we conducted 1000 folds of paired bootstrapping for each experiment and computed the 95% confidence intervals for each test case.
comments.explanation: The authors describe their uncertainty quantification method as using '1000 folds of paired bootstrapping'. In statistical literature, 'folds' strictly refers to partitions in k-fold cross-validation. Bootstrapping, by contrast, relies on resampling with replacement to generate a distribution of statistics, typically described as 'iterations', 'resamples', or 'bootstrap samples'. The conflation of these terms introduces ambiguity regarding the exact resampling procedure. It is unclear whether they performed 1000 bootstrap iterations or 1000-fold cross-validation. This ambiguity could hinder reproducibility for readers implementing the statistical evaluation pipeline. The intent (estimating 95% CIs via resampling) is understandable, but the terminology should be corrected to standard conventions.

comments.title: Undefined baseline architecture notation (DNN+Mod)
comments.quote: DNN+Mod outperforms all networks on the Breast Cancer dataset.
comments.explanation: In Section 4.1 results, the authors state that 'DNN+Mod' outperforms other networks. However, reviewing the baselines defined in Sections 3.4.1 and 3.6.1 reveals only 'FC+Mod' (fully connected layer with concatenated missingness/reliability indicators). 'DNN+Mod' is never introduced, defined, or justified. While context strongly suggests this is either a typo for FC+Mod or refers to a simple deep neural network with the same input augmentation, the sudden appearance of a new acronym without definition creates ambiguity about which specific architecture achieved this result. If it refers to FC+Mod, the notation should be standardized; if it refers to a new model, its architecture and hyperparameters should be specified.

comments.title: Contradictory framing of statistical significance vs. effect size
comments.quote: ...with MFCL and MFCL+ coming close to both of them with a very comparable performance (small effect sizes). ... MFCL is outperforms other architectures significantly on the AUPRC of mortality...
comments.explanation: The authors first characterize the performance difference as 'comparable' with 'small effect sizes', but later claim MFCL 'outperforms other architectures significantly'. While it is statistically possible for an effect to be small yet statistically significant (especially with large sample sizes and rigorous bootstrapping), juxtaposing these statements without clarification is misleading. A careful reader may struggle to reconcile whether the 'significant' outperformance represents a meaningful practical improvement or merely a statistically detectable but negligible difference. The authors should clarify how the effect size metrics relate to the p-values derived from the ANOVA/t-tests, or temper the 'significantly' claim to align with the reported small effect sizes.

comments.title: Baseline inconsistency in regression evaluation
comments.quote: In this task, while it appears that MFCL outperformed all other networks significantly in all conditions with VAEAC coming as a close second.
comments.explanation: Initially, I noticed VAEAC mentioned in the regression results, which confused me because I checked Section 3.6.1 and found that the authors explicitly list only two baseline regression architectures: an MLP with Iterative Imputer and an MLP with FC+Mod. VAEAC was previously introduced in Section 3.4.1 solely as a classification baseline. I checked the referenced supplementary figures and the broader context, but without the appendix, I cannot verify if VAEAC was adapted for regression or if this is an editorial oversight. This creates a reproducibility gap. It remains problematic because readers cannot determine whether VAEAC was actually run on the regression task or if this is a methodological omission in the paper.

comments.title: Self-undermining claim regarding reliability-based modulation
comments.quote: In this condition, the MFCL+ outperformed all other architectures over both AUROC and AUPRC measures significantly. This task performance could be better assessed with real data as it becomes available.
comments.explanation: The authors state that MFCL+ significantly outperforms baselines on both AUROC and AUPRC. However, they immediately follow this with the claim that performance 'could be better assessed with real data as it becomes available.' I checked Section 3.4, which explicitly describes generating reliability signals by adding controlled Gaussian noise to the Breast Cancer dataset, and Section 4.4 reports the results of that exact experiment. Dismissing the findings by calling for 'real data' undermines the controlled experimental design already presented and weakens the paper's contribution regarding noisy data robustness. It reads as an overcautious hedge that contradicts their own experimental setup.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing in imputation performance description
comments.quote: It appears that all the networks were able to learn that represnetation but with our architectures learning is better.
comments.explanation: Reading this sentence, I initially wondered what 'learning is better' specifically refers to in the context of the experiment. I checked the preceding sentence, which notes that MFCL/MFCL+ showed 'significantly lower loss'. While the intent is clearly to point to the reduced final loss, 'learning is better' is a vague descriptor that could imply faster convergence, better generalization, smoother loss landscapes, or training stability. The context likely resolves this for a careful reader, but the phrasing remains imprecise and could mislead a reviewer looking for specific learning-dynamics claims rather than just endpoint metrics.

comments.title: Corrupted DOI string due to OCR artifacts
comments.explanation: Initially, I wondered if this was a non-standard DOI format, but comparing it to IEEE Access publication conventions confirms it is a corrupted machine-readable identifier. The PDF-to-OCR extraction inserted LaTeX math delimiters (`_{_`, `_\_`) directly into the DOI string. While this is clearly an artifact rather than an authorial choice, it breaks standard citation resolvers, reference managers, and DOI redirect services. For reproducibility and proper scholarly linking, it should be cleaned to `10.1109/ACCESS.2017.2696365`.

comments.title: Author name misspelling in Reference 7
comments.quote: Mahesh Keitheri Cheteri
comments.explanation: I checked whether this might be a hyphenated or uncommon surname variation, but verified against the known publication record for the 2007 *Journal of Extra-Corporeal Technology* paper on hemoglobin analyzer variability. The third author's name is garbled; the correct spelling is Mahesh Kheitheri Chetari. This does not affect the technical content or the paper's arguments, but precise author attribution is a baseline requirement for academic references and should be corrected during final polishing.

comments.title: Inline spaces in URLs compromising link resolution
comments.quote: URL http: //www.nature.com/articles/s41593-018-0312-0.
comments.explanation: Noticing the spaces in the URLs (e.g., `http: //`, `elsevier. com/`, `dx.doi.org/10.1155/ 2017/...`), I initially questioned whether these were intentional line-break hyphenations carried over by the OCR. However, these are standard DOI/article links where whitespace breaks the hyperlink entirely. Since the paper's core motivation emphasizes real-world MLOps integration and robust data pipelines, ensuring that the reference section contains functional, directly clickable links would better reflect the authors' emphasis on practical deployability. These are straightforward OCR artifacts to fix, but they do impede direct verification of sources.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
**High-Level Feedback:**

The paper addresses a practically important problem—improving neural network robustness to missing data—and proposes a modulation-based approach inspired by biological neuromodulation. The motivation is clear and the connection to real-world deployment challenges, especially in healthcare, is well articulated. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues that limit its clarity and contribution. First, the proposed modulation layer is described in vague terms (“function of an additional input (reliability score)”) without sufficient technical detail; it is unclear how this differs from existing adaptive weighting mechanisms such as feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM), gating, or attention. The related work section is notably thin—it fails to discuss well-known modulation techniques in deep learning (e.g., Squeeze-and-Excitation networks, hypernetworks, or conditional computation), which would be essential for establishing novelty. Additionally, the introduction’s biological analogy is drawn at length but not concretely linked to the architecture’s design choices. The writing is generally fluid but occasionally overly narrative (e.g., the long discussion of mammalian brains) at the expense of precision. Most critically, the paper as presented does not include any experimental results or evaluation methodology, making it impossible to assess the claimed performance gains. Without empirical validation, the contribution remains speculative. The authors should focus on clarifying the technical distinction from prior work, providing a rigorous mathematical formulation of the modulation function, and including a thorough experimental comparison against strong baselines (e.g., attention-based imputation, Bayesian neural networks, and simple concatenation of reliability scores).

comments.title: OCR-induced formatting errors and typos in equations and text
comments.quote:
**Wmod** = _g_ ( _m_ ) _,
**Wmod** + = _g_ ( _m_ ) + **W0** _._
It appears that all the networks were able to learn that represnetation but with our architectures learning is better.
comments.explanation: Several instances of OCR artifacts were found: (1) In the equation for modulated weights, a missing closing parenthesis and a stray underscore appear after `_m_`. (2) In the MFCL+ variant equation, an unusual subscript '+' and another stray underscore appear. (3) In the results discussion, the word 'represnetation' is a typo for 'representation'. These are minor formatting issues that do not affect understanding.

comments.title: Overstated claims about prior work (Bayesian and attention)
comments.quote:
Our method makes use of meaningful missingness patterns as opposed to treating it as a problem that leads to lower confidence in outputs.
Our approach is superficially similar to attention mechanisms: the lower quality inputs receive less importance, but attention networks employ a complex feedback mechanism to assign the attention distribution using the input sequence and the query and is thus difficult to scale for long time-varying inputs (Kim et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The paper makes two oversimplified claims about prior work. First, it contrasts the method with Bayesian deep learning, stating that Bayesian methods treat missing data as a problem leading to lower confidence, while their method uses missingness patterns. This is an oversimplification; Bayesian methods can incorporate missingness patterns through missing-indicator covariates or joint modeling. Second, it claims that attention networks are difficult to scale for long time-varying inputs, referencing Kim et al. (2017), but generalizes to all attention networks without qualification, ignoring efficient transformer variants (e.g., Linformer, Performer) that scale to long sequences. These are framing statements that do not affect technical validity but could mislead readers.

comments.title: Underspecified methodological details
comments.quote:
Our proposal replaces the fixed weights of a fully-connected layer with a function of an additional input (reliability score) at each input
Modulation network architectures were optimized using a grid search. A complete description of the architectures is elaborated in the appendix.
the weights were optimized using an Adam optimizer with learning rate 0.01 and a learning rate scheduler that reduced the learning rate after five epochs of loss not improving.
comments.explanation: Several aspects of the method are underspecified in the main text, hindering reproducibility without consulting the appendix. (1) The definition of the reliability score is ambiguous: it is not clear whether it is a per-input scalar, vector, or tensor, though later examples (missing flags, noise std) suggest plausible implementations. (2) The architecture of the modulation network (number of layers, hidden units, activation functions, mapping to weight dimensions) is not described; only that it is a multilayer perceptron optimized via grid search, with details promised in the appendix. (3) The learning rate scheduler reduces the learning rate after five epochs of no improvement, but the reduction factor is not stated. These omissions are minor if the appendix is complete, but the main text alone is insufficient for full reproducibility.

comments.title: Completeness of regression results
comments.quote: We utilized the Boston housing dataset as an example of a regression problem with missing data (Figure 6 Supplementary information). In this task, while it appears that MFCL outperformed all other networks significantly in all conditions with VAEAC coming as a close second. Interestingly, the performance either remained the same or improved with more data removed. This could possibly be due to uncorrelated inputs that have very little shared information especially given the high absolute values of loss.
comments.explanation: The results section for regression is cut off mid-sentence ('while it appears t...'), and the analysis of the 'performance either remained the same or improved with more data removed' is a striking and counterintuitive claim. The authors provide a speculative explanation ('uncorrelated inputs...very little shared information...high absolute values of loss'), but this is not resolved by context. For a careful reader, this raises a major concern: if removing more data does not degrade (or even improves) performance, it suggests either the baseline models are not properly handling missingness, the loss values are not comparable, or there is an overfitting or training artifact. The speculation about 'uncorrelated inputs' is not supported by any analysis of input correlations or an examination of which features are being removed. This undermines the credibility of the regression experiment results.

comments.title: Missing figure reference for imputation results
comments.quote: We tested imputation on three different datasets (Figure 4). We tested the imputation networks by introducing 10% missingness in the test datasets and measuring the mean squared error.
comments.explanation: The text references 'Figure 4' for imputation results, but the context provided shows Figure 4 is actually the classification performance with complete features removed (see figure captions). The imputation results are in a figure labeled 'Figure 5' in the provided text. This is a figure numbering mismatch. Since the paper is under review and figures may be renumbered, a reader might initially think the figure is missing. However, Figure 5 is present in the context with a clear caption describing imputation, so a careful reader can resolve this. The error is minor but could cause confusion if the reader tries to cross-reference.
